START.text: End of day. Let's look at it.

Q1.text: Something didn't go the way you wanted today. Before you had time to think about it — what was your first instinct?

options.text: To figure out what I missed or did wrong.
To think about what made it harder than it needed to be.
To wonder whether it was even a fair situation to be in.
To move past it and focus on what was next.

Q2_I.text: When you hit an obstacle you didn't anticipate today, what did you actually do?

options.text: Adapted — figured out a workaround or changed my approach.
Pushed through — kept doing what I was doing but harder.
Paused — asked for input before deciding how to move.
Noted it — didn't address it yet but plan to.

Q2_E.text: When you think about what made today harder — how clearly can you name it right now?

options.text: Completely clearly — I can name exactly what or who the blocker was.
Somewhat — I know something was off but not precisely what.
Vaguely — it just felt like friction without a specific cause.
I'd rather not examine it today.

REFLECT_LOCUS_INT.text: You went looking for your own hand in what happened. That's not self-blame — it's the starting position for anyone who wants to learn from a day rather than just survive it.

REFLECT_LOCUS_EXT.text: When things go sideways, attention goes outward first. That's a real response to a real situation. Somewhere in there, though, you made at least one call. You don't have to find it tonight.

BRIDGE_1_2.text: Now let's shift — from how you handled things, to what you were oriented toward.

Q3.text: Pick something you actually finished today — anything. Not the most important thing. Just something done. What crosses your mind when you look at it now?

options.text: Whether it actually moved something forward for someone.
That it's done — there's a real satisfaction in closing things out.
Whether anyone noticed, or will.
What it unlocks or enables next.

Q4_C.text: When your work creates value for others — how does that actually affect your sense of whether the day was worth it?

options.text: It's the primary measure. Without that, even a productive day feels hollow.
It matters, but I also need to feel personally satisfied with the quality.
It depends on who benefited — some people matter more to me than others.
I don't think about it that way — I just focus on the work itself.

Q4_E.text: When you think about what you're owed for your effort today — recognition, opportunity, a clearer path — where are you?

options.text: Not thinking about it — I'm focused on what I'm building.
There's a gap between what I've contributed and what I've received.
I've been doing more than others at this level and it hasn't been noticed.
I deserve a clearer path forward and haven't been given one.

REFLECT_ORIENT_C.text: You're measuring today by what it moved for someone else. That's not a given — most people measure by what got finished, what got recognized, or what got them further along.

REFLECT_ORIENT_E.text: You're aware of what the day cost you relative to what it gave back. That accounting is worth doing honestly — it tells you something about where you are, not just what happened.

BRIDGE_2_3.text: Last axis. This one's about range — how wide your awareness actually was today.

Q5.text: Right now, at the end of the day — who else is in the room in your head?

options.text: Someone specific — a person I'm still thinking about.
The team in some general sense — I'm tracking how we're doing.
Mostly just me. I needed the focus, and I'm okay with that.
No one, really. Today was heads-down and that's how it is.

Q6_HIGH.text: When you noticed someone else having a hard time today — what did you actually do with that?

options.text: Acted on it — checked in, offered something, adjusted.
Held it — I was aware but deliberate about when and how to engage.
Noted it — intended to follow up but didn't get there.
I don't have a consistent pattern here.

Q6_LOW.text: When you reflect on a day where you were very focused on your own work — what's the texture of that?

options.text: Useful — I got a lot done and I needed that concentrated time.
Slightly uncomfortable — I wonder if I missed something important.
Normal — most of my days look like this and I don't question it.
Relieving — less input from others means less complexity.

REFLECT_RADIUS_HIGH.text: You carried someone else today — in your awareness, at minimum. That's not nothing. Whether it turned into action is a separate question.

REFLECT_RADIUS_LOW.text: Today was contained. Just you, your work, your own field of view. Some days that's a choice. Some days it's a default. Worth knowing which one this was.

SUMMARY_ICH.title: The Integrated Agent
SUMMARY_ICH.text:
You took responsibility for what went wrong, measured your work by what it did for someone else, and kept other people in your field of awareness. That combination is rarer than it sounds — most people do one or two of those on any given day.

The version of this that goes wrong looks like someone so calibrated to others that their own signal gets lost in the noise. You probably don't have that problem, but it's worth checking: when you're reading the room, are you also in it?

What would you still do tomorrow if no one were paying attention?

SUMMARY_ICL.title: The Productive Isolate
SUMMARY_ICL.text:
You took ownership of today and thought about impact — but the day was mostly yours. Not in an avoidant way; just contained. The work moved forward on your terms, in your space, toward something you believe in.

The risk here isn't low performance — it's miscalibration. You can be producing real value and still be misaligned with what the team or the organization actually needs, and not find out for a long time because you're not in a position to hear it.

High-quality output in a closed loop is still a closed loop.

SUMMARY_IEH.title: The Aware Deficit Tracker
SUMMARY_IEH.text:
You saw other people today — probably clearly — and you also felt the gap between what you've put in and what you've gotten back. Those two things coexist without contradiction; awareness of others doesn't automatically produce satisfaction with your situation.

The pattern to watch: when the deficit becomes the dominant frame, social awareness becomes a form of scorekeeping — who got recognized, who didn't, who got the opportunity. That's not cynicism. It's a coherent response to an environment you've correctly diagnosed. But diagnosis without traction turns into inventory.

What would you do with this situation if you stopped waiting for someone to notice the gap?

SUMMARY_IEL.title: The Self-Referential Agent
SUMMARY_IEL.text:
You owned today. Took responsibility. But the frame was almost entirely yours — your trajectory, your standards, your assessment of how you performed. The day wasn't about others, and you didn't sit in a posture of waiting to be rewarded. It was simply about you doing the work you're trying to do.

That's a disciplined and often productive state. It can also be a form of insulation — against feedback, against the messiness of actually needing others, against the possibility that your definition of 'good work' and theirs are different.

Self-accountability that doesn't include others isn't accountability — it's preference management.

SUMMARY_ECH.title: The Generous Attributor
SUMMARY_ECH.text:
You were oriented toward impact and aware of the people around you — both genuinely hard to do under friction. But when something went wrong today, the causation pointed outward. That's a real asymmetry: you assign ownership of value creation internally, but ownership of setbacks goes external.

The asymmetry isn't dishonest — it may be accurate. But contribution-oriented people who consistently attribute setbacks to external causes tend, over time, to become frustrated. They're giving a lot, they see the people they're serving, and they watch obstacles pile up in a space they feel they can't touch.

What would change if you treated the obstacles as part of the work, not interruptions to it?

SUMMARY_ECL.title: The Disconnected Contributor
SUMMARY_ECL.text:
You intended impact today — the framing was outward — but you were largely heads-down, and when something went wrong, the cause was somewhere else. The combination produces a particular kind of frustration: you're trying to contribute, the environment keeps getting in the way, and you're too deep in the work to notice whether you're actually landing.

This isn't a character problem. It's a feedback problem. You don't have enough signal about whether what you're doing is connecting with anyone — because you're not in a position to receive it.

Contribution without calibration eventually becomes effort without direction.

SUMMARY_EEH.title: The Sensitized Observer
SUMMARY_EEH.text:
You read the room well today — saw people, tracked dynamics, understood what was happening around you. And you felt something was owed that hasn't arrived. What's notable is the combination: high perceptual accuracy, low sense of personal leverage.

This is often a correct diagnosis of a genuinely unfair situation. It's also a state that tends to be self-reinforcing — sharp perception of what's wrong, without the internal framing to see where you have any hand in changing it, produces increasingly detailed evidence of a problem you can't solve.

What you can see precisely, you can't always fix. But the question is whether that stops at diagnosis.

SUMMARY_EEL.title: The Closed Circuit
SUMMARY_EEL.text:
The day happened to you, and you were mostly thinking about your own position in it. There's no aggression in that — it's just what the day looked like. Setbacks came from outside, work was about you, and other people existed at the periphery.

This is the state most resistant to reflection — not because you're unwilling, but because the feedback loops are self-confirming. External causation means nothing is yours to change. Self-focus means the signal from others doesn't arrive. Without either, there's nothing for today's reflection to act on.

When everything happens to you and everything is about you — what would it even mean for something to be your fault?

locus.INTERNAL_SHALLOW: Note: you persisted today rather than adapted. Those aren't the same thing.
locus.EXTERNAL_DIFFUSE: The obstacle you felt today didn't have a clear shape. That makes it harder to do anything about — and easier to carry forward.
locus.EXTERNAL_AVOIDANT: You'd rather not look at it today. That's a valid response to a hard day. It's also how patterns become invisible.

orientation.CONTRIBUTION_SELECTIVE: Your contribution orientation is conditional — some people count more than others. That's honest to say. It's also worth knowing.
orientation.CONTRIBUTION_INTRINSIC: Your orientation is toward craft, not impact. That's a legitimate way to work. It's also a different thing than contribution.
orientation.ENTITLEMENT_PERCEIVED_GAP: You've calculated a gap between effort and return. The calculation may be accurate. But living inside it is a choice.
orientation.ENTITLEMENT_COMPARATIVE: You're measuring your position against others'. That comparison will never close — someone will always have gotten more.
orientation.ENTITLEMENT_FRUSTRATED: You want a clearer path and haven't been given one. That frustration is real. It's also preventing you from building one.

radius.RADIUS_PASSIVE: You noticed, and you meant to follow up. The gap between noticing and acting is where most relational debt accumulates.
radius.RADIUS_UNCERTAIN: You don't have a consistent pattern here. Inconsistency in this domain usually means you're reacting to your own state, not responding to theirs.
radius.SELF_REFLECTIVE: The discomfort you felt about being inward-facing is the most useful thing in today's session. Follow it.
radius.SELF_DEFAULT: Most of your days look like this and you don't question it. That sentence is worth questioning.
radius.SELF_PROTECTIVE: You felt relief when others weren't in the mix. Worth sitting with — not as a judgment, just as a fact about how you're wired right now.
